def: At the apex; pertaining to the tip of an appendage or sclerite.
A keel-like or sharply raised ridge on a sclerite. Plural: carinae.
The plate of the head between the labrum and the frons; often used as a key character.
Heart-shaped; said of a pronotum widest at or near the anterior third and narrowed posteriorly.
Bearing one or more raised longitudinal ribs (costae), as on the elytra of some Carabus.
The hardened, opaque forewings of beetles; meet medially along the elytral suture and protect the membranous hindwings.
Spread out and flattened, as the lateral margins of the pronotum or elytra.
Rust-coloured; reddish-brown.
Thread-like; said of antennae with subequal, slender segments.
The anterior part of the head between the eyes, above the clypeus.
Elbowed; said of antennae bent at an angle, common in weevils.
Smooth, without setae or punctures.
The lateral, downturned portion of the pronotum visible from below.
The 'upper lip' of the mouthparts, articulating with the clypeus.
Plate-like; said of antennae whose terminal segments form expanded, often closable plates (Scarabaeidae).
The paired, sclerotized cutting jaws; their shape is highly diagnostic.
Paired mouthparts behind the mandibles, bearing palps and used in food manipulation.
The middle thoracic segment, bearing the mesonotum, mesosternum, and the elytra.
The ventral plate of the metathorax.
The dorsal plate of the prothorax — the visible 'shield' between head and elytra.
Bearing the mouthparts directed forward in line with the body axis.
Bearing impressed pits or dots (punctures).
A tooth-like process on the inner edge of a mandible, used to grip prey.
Wrinkled or coarsely sculpted.
A small, often triangular sclerite at the base of the elytra, formed by the mesonotum.
Saw-toothed; said of antennal segments with lateral projections.
A bristle or hair. Plural: setae.
Longitudinal grooves or lines, especially on the elytra; often bear punctures (punctate striae).
The terminal segments of the leg; tarsal formula (e.g. 5-5-5) gives the segment count on the pro-, meso-, and metathoracic legs.
The fourth leg segment, between femur and tarsi.
